segments.title: A long-range flying boat from Blackburn's oversized-aircraft experience
segments.summary: Blackburn drew on its earlier large-aircraft work when it began the R.B.1 Iris, a very large long-range reconnaissance flying boat designed to Air Ministry Specification R.14/24.
segments.body: The Iris is introduced as evidence that Blackburn could produce an elegant aircraft as well as more familiar carrier types. Work began in 1924, with Major J. D. Rennie designing the large flying boat for long-range reconnaissance. The brief and its scale show why the Iris belonged to a different operational problem from a short-range deck aircraft: it needed to operate far from base while remaining viable at sea.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the Iris, Blackburn's previous large-aircraft experience, its 1924 start, Rennie, and Specification R.14/24.

segments.title: Three engines for a safer overwater patrol
segments.summary: The Iris used three engines to reduce the danger of a forced descent at sea, retaining altitude at full load on two engines and remaining airborne after losing two.
segments.body: For a long-range flying boat, engine redundancy was a practical maritime safety feature rather than just a performance statistic. Dr. Clarke explains that the Iris could maintain height fully loaded with one engine out, and could still continue after a second loss, albeit at a lower altitude. That reserve mattered because a forced descent far from shore could turn an engine failure into an immediate survival problem.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the three-engine arrangement, its forced-descent rationale, and the aircraft's performance after one or two engine failures.

segments.title: Hull form and the difficult transition to flight
segments.summary: The Iris combined a plywood-covered wooden hull with a sharp two-step V-bottom to run cleanly on the water and shorten the costly, demanding takeoff transition.
segments.body: The Iris's hull was designed for clean water handling, using plywood-covered wood construction and a sharp two-step V-bottom. Dr. Clarke connects that shape directly to the takeoff problem: an aircraft must build speed and lift while engines run at full power, consuming fuel and imposing strain. A hull that gets the aircraft through the water phase quickly reduces the period when takeoff and landing are most demanding. He closes by noting the Iris's rarity: only four were built.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the Iris hull, explains why fast water acceleration matters for takeoff, and notes that only four aircraft were built.
